commonIssues: Overwatering and poor drainage are the biggest cause of decline and death; let the soil dry between waterings.
Root and crown rot plus botrytis (grey mould) are the most common diseases; ensure good air circulation and free-draining media.

Frost-sensitive: hardy only to about -8C (18F); protect from hard frost and cold wet winters, or overwinter container plants indoors in cold zones (not reliably hardy below zone 8 except cultivars like 'Arp').
Common pests include aphids, spider mites, mealybugs and scale; rosemary beetle and leafhoppers in some regions.
Container minimum is set to a realistic pot of at least 30cm (12in) wide and deep per RHS; smaller pots dry out fast and restrict the woody root system.
Reduce or stop watering in winter dormancy; overwatering wet cold roots is the main winter killer.

source.citation: RHS - How to grow Rosemary (Grow Your Own): full sun, light free-draining soil, hardy to -8C (18F), drought tolerant once established, container minimum 30cm wide and deep with drainage, feed container plants over one year old with general-purpose granular feed in late spring/early summer, problems include root rot from waterlogging, rosemary beetle, scale and leafhoppers.

RHS - How to grow Rosemary: minimum container 30cm (1ft) wide and deep with drainage holes using a soil-based compost; heavy soils need up to 25% horticultural grit. A 30cm-wide/deep pot corresponds to roughly 20-30 L; recommendedMinLiters set conservatively to 15 L.
RHS - How to grow Rosemary: hardy to -8C (18F); harsh frosts and cold winds cause damage and young plants may die in extreme cold; needs winter protection in colder/wetter sites.

RHS - How to grow Rosemary: ground plants need no feeding; container plants over one year old benefit from a sprinkling of general-purpose granular feed in late spring or early summer (about once per season), informing the ~60-day base feeding interval and low-fertility preference.

University of Wisconsin-Madison Horticulture Extension - Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis): keep soil moist with good drainage; overwatering is the biggest cause of plant decline and death; winter hardy only to approximately 20F (cultivar-dependent, 'Arp' to ~10F); root rot and botrytis are the most common diseases; pests include aphids, spider mites, mealybugs and scale; good air circulation prevents foliar disease.

University of Illinois Extension - Rosemary: full sun; keep the soil on the dry side and let it dry between waterings; overwatering causes root rot; perennial in USDA zone 8 and above, not reliably hardy in zone 6 or colder ('Arp' potentially to zone 6); bring indoors before frost.
University of Illinois Extension - Rosemary: perennial in zone 8 and above, not reliably hardy in zone 6 or colder; informs the zone-8 spring (Mar-May) planting window and dormancy guidance.

Multiple extension/horticulture sources concur rosemary needs full sun, at least 6-8 hours of direct sunlight per day for best growth in containers and the garden (e.g. University of Illinois Extension and RHS specify 'full sun'; 6-8 hours is the widely cited full-sun figure for this Mediterranean shrub).

Texas A&M AgriLife Extension - Easy Gardening: Rosemary: plant in full sun in well-drained soil; avoid overwatering as too much water or poor drainage causes problems. Corroborates full-sun, free-draining, drought-tolerant care and high overwater risk.

Confidence note: exact numeric values for baseIntervalDays (7), hotWeatherMultiplier (0.7), rainSkipThresholdMm (5), heatStressC (35), feeding baseIntervalDays (60), containerLeachAdjustment (0.75), and recommendedMinLiters (15) are conservative app-model estimates derived from qualitative source guidance (full sun, 'keep on the dry side', drought-tolerant, water container plants ~weekly, 30cm pot, feed ~once per season). No single extension page gives a numeric watering interval, heat-stress temperature, or pot volume in liters; these are engineered conservatively to avoid overwatering this rot-prone species.
